claims.evidence_text: A 9-year study found that the yield of wheat under NT was equivalent to or better than that under conventional tillage (Mrabet, 2011), while El Mzouri et al. (2023) showed similar results in contrasting climatic contexts.
claims.claim_summary: Over a 9-year study, wheat yield under no-till (NT) was equivalent to or better than under conventional tillage.

claims.evidence_text: Non-tillage demonstrated higher ear density and increased durum wheat grain yield compared to other systems, particularly in dry years.
claims.claim_summary: No-till produced higher ear (spike) density and greater durum wheat grain yield than minimum tillage or conventional tillage, especially in dry years.
claims.management_system: no-till (NT) vs minimum tillage vs conventional tillage

claims.evidence_text: Long term trials conﬁrm that NT exceeds conventional tillage in terms of yield, that crop rotation improves stability and increases wheat yields, and that NT-rotation maximizes energy eﬃciency while being more cost-eﬀective and less risky (Mrabet, 2008).
claims.claim_summary: Crop rotation improves yield stability and increases wheat yields; no-till combined with rotation maximizes energy efficiency.

claims.evidence_text: It was suggested that tillage systems could eﬀectively inﬂuence soil water availability, thereby impacting wheat yield.
claims.claim_summary: Tillage system choice influences soil water availability and thereby winter wheat yield under rainfed conditions in northwest coastal Egypt.
claims.management_system: ridge/furrow, tank, conventional, and no-till tillage systems
